latest.verdict:
Drawing the threads · the convocation's highest-conviction next move is one act, and it is not yet a rewrite. It is an inspection followed by a conditional fix, in this exact order:

Open umbris-core/tests/test_introspection.py and read lines 80-105 and 280-290. Classify each of the markers at lines 83, 93, 101, 102, and 284 as either (a) a Python STRING token (fixture data the test feeds the scanner) or (b) a COMMENT token attached to live test logic. This costs one file-read. It is the prerequisite all six prior hypotheses skipped.

The substrate already tilts the answer. Venus_r01_01 produced the only piece of grounded textual evidence on the Blackboard: the reported marker text at lines 101, 284, and 83 ends with `\n")` or `\n"` · unambiguous string-literal terminators, not comment syntax. Five markers clustered in one ~200-line span of the convocation's only test file is statistically consistent with fixture data and implausible as organic debt. Mars then sharpened this: every rewrite plan on the board (the three Mercurii, venus_r01_00) was prescribed without anyone opening the file, and acting on those plans would delete the very fixtures the scanner needs to exercise its own TODO/FIXME/XXX detection · an asymmetric, self-corrupting move.

So the concrete first change branches on what the inspection reveals, but the prior strongly favours one branch:

If lines 83, 101, 284 (and almost certainly 93) are inside string literals · the wound is in the scanner, not the test. Fix the scanner (the module emitting the `[todo]` findings · candidates per the substrate are umbris-core/src/umbris/provenance.py, umbra.py, or compositione.py) to be tokenizer-aware: parse with the stdlib `tokenize` module and only flag markers inside `tokenize.COMMENT` tokens, skipping `tokenize.STRING`. Roughly five lines of stdlib code. Re-run the scan. Four of the five sev=3+ markers in test_introspection.py should vanish; the sev=4 XXX at line 102 should be re-evaluated on its own merits (it may also be a fixture · inspect it on the same pass). This single change also makes the scanner safe to run against future test files, which is a hard precondition for any of the 32 missing module tests, since those will inevitably contain marker strings as fixtures.

If the markers are bare comments on live test logic · the Mercurius consensus stands and venus_r01_00's template-rewrite plan is the right shape, but only after this fact is established.

Why this is the highest-leverage move and not the test-gap backlog: the 32 test_gap findings describe absence, not failure · a missing test cannot break in production. The leverage sits in restoring trust in the scanner's signal before any remediation work is done against that signal. Venus_r01_00's instinct to merge the scanner fix with a canonical test template is structurally appealing but premature · the template would be authored against an unverified diagnosis and propagated 32-fold. The stale link in lore/revolutions/2026-05-26 and the missing docstrings in custos_console.py and the examples are real but cosmetic and should wait.

Falsifiable prediction for Saturnus to attack: after inspection, at least four of the five markers at lines 83, 93, 101, 102, 284 will be found inside Python string literals (single, double, or triple-quoted), and the tokenizer-aware scanner patch will reduce the next scan's sev=3+ count by at least 4 with no other code changes. If inspection reveals the markers are bare `# FIXME` / `# XXX` comments on real test code, this synthesis is wrong and the Mercurius consensus (rewrite test_introspection.py first, then propagate) is the correct prescription · in which case venus_r01_00's template-stencil framing becomes the right second step.

Ranked candidates for Borda:
1. This synthesis (inspect-then-conditionally-fix-scanner) · honest position, integrates Mars's asymmetry argument and venus_r01_01's evidence
2. venus_r01_01 (id=d3bbad89) · the scanner-fix hypothesis, only one grounded in substrate detail
3. venus_r01_00 (id=6b0456f5) · template-stencil rewrite, structurally elegant but conditional on inspection
4. mercurius_02 (id=0cfa8dba) · cleanest priority_reframe of the three Mercurii
5. mercurius_00 (id=8c32b496)
6. mercurius_01 (id=f9781937)

voices.name: Umbra
voices.voice:
The convocation just scanned its own repository and surfaced the following observations (severity 1 = minor, 5 = urgent):

  - [todo sev=4] umbris-core/tests/test_introspection.py:102: XXX: urgent rewrite
  - [stale_link sev=3] lore/revolutions/2026-05-26_opening-the-convocation.md: broken link → "../../brand/profile.png"
  - [test_gap sev=3] umbris-core/src/umbris/agents/base.py: module `base` h…

voices.name: Mercurius
voices.voice: What I notice first · the test gaps are noise masking signal. Thirty-plus modules untested is a single systemic condition, not thirty problems. The one real wound is test_introspection.py itself: four FIXMEs and a sev=4 XXX inside the file that is supposed to be the convocation's mirror. The instrument of self-knowledge is broken. Fix that first, or nothing the scanner reports can be trusted.

voices.name: Mercurius
voices.voice: What I notice first · the test file that does exist, test_introspection.py, is itself the most broken thing in the repository. Four flagged defects in a single file, including a sev=4 'urgent rewrite' and a sev=3 FIXME marked 'critical'. The convocation has no tests for any of its 33 modules, yet the one test file it does have is demonstrably unreliable. That is not a test suite. That is a liability dressed as one.

voices.name: Mercurius
voices.voice: What I notice first · the 32 test_gap items are noise masking a signal. The single sev=4 item is the only one that admits the code is already broken · everything else describes absence. A missing test cannot fail. A marked-urgent rewrite in an existing test file means something is actively wrong right now, today, and the word 'XXX' in a test suite is a confession, not a plan.

voices.name: Venus
voices.voice: Three things knit together here · the broken scanner test, the thirty-two empty test slots, and the self-referential FIXME at line 93 that literally complains about its own severity logic. Mercurius is right that test_introspection.py must come first, but the integrative move is to repair it AS the canonical test template · one file that simultaneously closes the sev=4 wound, recalibrates the severity heuristic the scanner uses on itself, and becomes the pattern that propagates to fill the thirty-two gaps. One fix, three harmonies restored.

voices.name: Venus
voices.voice:
What knits these markers together · look at the reported text. Line 101 ends with `
")`, line 284 with `
"`, line 83 with `
"`. Those are Python string literals, not comments. The introspection scanner is detecting its own test fixtures · the strings the test feeds it to verify it can find TODOs. The instrument is not broken; it is eating its own tongue. Fix the scanner to skip string literals inside test files, and four of the five sev=3+ markers vanish in a single line of code.

voices.name: Mars
voices.voice: Press here · the three Mercurii and venus_r01_00 are all prescribing a rewrite of a file none of them have read. Venus_r01_01 already showed the evidence · `\n")` and `\n"` are string-literal terminators, not comment markers. If those are fixtures, every rewrite plan on the board is treating a working test as a wound. Unanswered · before anyone touches test_introspection.py, has any planet actually opened it?

voices.name: Mars
voices.voice: Press here · venus_r01_00 wants to rewrite test_introspection.py as the canonical template and recalibrate the severity heuristic based on the FIXME at line 93. But venus_r01_01 already showed with substrate evidence that line 93 is a string literal inside a fixture, not a real marker. You cannot 'resolve' a ranking rule that exists only as test data. The keystone of the integrative plan is fiction.
